evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a patch on pgsql-hackers that adds a new GUC plus a new inline helper function in src/include/utils/. What should I check?
evals.with_skill:
Run the seven-phase review in order, cheap first.

Phase 1 (Submission, 5 min): patch is unified/context diff with context lines, filename vN-0001-*.patch, applies cleanly to current master via `git checkout master && git pull && git am vN-*.patch`, includes tests under src/test/ (regress test that SET/SHOW the new GUC), includes doc update under doc/src/sgml/config.sgml. If any miss, kick back as Waiting on Author before reading further.

Phase 2 (Usability): cover-email goal matches diff. GUC name consistent with neighboring GUCs (snake_case, sensible category, plural/singular convention, unit suffix). Bounds, GUC_LIST_* flags, GUC_UNIT_* set right. Sensible interactions with parallel query, replication, extensions. Header is in src/include/utils/ — is it the right home, or should it live next to its users?

Phase 3 (Feature test): build with -Dcassert=true -Ddebug=true, `ninja && meson test`. No new warnings, all existing tests pass. New tests should fail without the code change (revert just the code part and re-run). Exercise corners: postgresql.conf, ALTER SYSTEM, SET LOCAL, SET inside subtransaction with rollback, SIGHUP reload, invalid values, NULL, max length. Assertion crash-test.

Phase 4 (Performance): inline helper on a hot path? pgbench baseline vs patched. Any new O(N^2)? Any per-row palloc that should use a short-lived MemoryContext?

Phase 5 (Coding): style matches surrounding module (CamelCase vs snake_case stays consistent), no long lines, comments explain WHY not WHAT, uses ereport/palloc/MemoryContext/ResourceOwner not parallel reinventions, error messages follow style guide (lowercase errmsg, no trailing period, errdetail/errhint separated), no new -Wall -Wextra warnings, no platform-specific code without portability gating.

Phase 6 (Architecture): locking order consistent with rest of system. Inline function in src/include/ is part of the ABI surface — back-branch ABI rules apply (new members at end of structs, no signature changes on exported functions, new enum values at end). Catalog change? If yes, CATALOG_VERSION_NO bump and master-only. Does the GUC foreclose future extensions (e.g. could it need to become a list later)? pg_dump support not needed for a GUC, but would be for any new schema object.

Phase 7 (Review review): blocking issues vs nits clearly separated, asks are concrete ("please add a test for SET LOCAL inside a subtransaction that rolls back" beats "more tests please"). Post inline plain-text on pgsql-hackers, preserve threading, flip CF entry to Waiting on Author (if blocking) or Ready for Committer.
evals.baseline: Check that the GUC name is sensible and consistent with siblings. Verify there's a doc update in config.sgml. Verify a test exercises SET/SHOW. Confirm patch applies, builds clean, tests pass. Read the inline function — does it actually need to be inline, and is it small enough to justify being in a header? Check the GUC's category, bounds, unit, and that the assign/check hooks (if any) handle errors via ereport. Make sure no thread-safety or SIGHUP-reload issue. Look for style consistency with surrounding code.

evals.prompt: A CommitFest patch touches WAL and adds a new XLOG record type for a new index AM. What's my review checklist for it?
evals.with_skill:
Run the seven phases with extra weight on Phase 6.

Phase 1 (Submission): vN-0001-*.patch, applies to master, tests present (must include crash-recovery TAP test under src/test/recovery/, not just regress), docs updated if user-visible (new AM, contrib README, etc.).

Phase 2 (Usability): cover-letter claim matches diff. New AM registered correctly in pg_am. Interactions: logical replication (does the new record need a logical decoding callback or is it irrelevant?), physical replication, parallel query.

Phase 3 (Feature test): assertion build mandatory. `meson test` (regress + isolation + recovery). Crash-recovery test: build state, SIGKILL postmaster, restart, verify recovery replays. Revert just the redo function and re-run — new TAP test must fail. Verify pg_waldump prints the record sensibly via rm_desc/rm_identify.

Phase 4 (Performance): WAL volume regression on existing workloads? Per-tuple WAL allocation? FPI rules right with XLogRegisterBuffer / REGBUF_* flags?

Phase 5 (Coding): redo function uses XLogReadBufferForRedo idiom, no palloc inside critical section, START_CRIT_SECTION / END_CRIT_SECTION bracketing correct, error messages style-correct, no new warnings.

Phase 6 (Architecture — the deep one for WAL):
  - XLOG_PAGE_MAGIC bumped (mandatory for any WAL format change).
  - New RMID registered in src/include/access/rmgrlist.h with rm_redo / rm_desc / rm_identify / rm_startup / rm_cleanup / rm_mask callbacks.
  - rm_desc and rm_identify implemented so pg_waldump works.
  - Backwards compatibility: master-only, never backpatchable.
  - RM ID range: built-in needs a newly assigned reserved ID; out-of-tree AM should use RM_EXPERIMENTAL_ID.
  - Locking order in redo matches normal-path lock acquisition order — out-of-order = recovery deadlock.
  - Hint-bit rules: MarkBufferDirtyHint vs MarkBufferDirty correctness in redo.
  - Buffer pin / content-lock interactions preserved.
  - If on-disk format also changed: pg_upgrade path or version-gate.
  - Catalog: new pg_am row → CATALOG_VERSION_NO bump, OID assignment, master-only.
  - Cross-check `knowledge/architecture/wal.md` and the wal-and-xlog skill for the full WAL-record checklist.

Phase 7: blocking vs nits, concrete asks, plain-text reply, flip CF state.
evals.baseline: Bump XLOG_PAGE_MAGIC; register a resource manager in rmgrlist.h with redo/desc/identify and the other callbacks; implement the redo function with no palloc inside the critical section and use XLogReadBufferForRedo; add pg_waldump support via rm_desc/rm_identify; add a crash-recovery TAP test under src/test/recovery/; verify physical replication still works; think about logical decoding; check buffer-locking order in redo matches the write path; assign an RM ID in the right range; if a new pg_am row, bump CATALOG_VERSION_NO and assign an OID — master-only, not backpatchable. Also check FPI rules with REGBUF_* flags.

evals.prompt: Pre-submission review: my own patch claims a 30% speedup on hash join probes by reordering a hot loop. How do I verify the claim before sending to hackers?
evals.with_skill:
Pre-submission self-review: run the same seven phases, ruthlessly, with Phase 4 as the focus.

Phase 1: patch applies clean to current master, vN-0001-*.patch filename, tests present, doc updates if user-visible (a perf-only patch likely just needs a commit-message line, possibly a release-note hint).

Phase 2: claim in cover letter must match diff exactly (workload, query shape, numbers). Write a precise repro recipe — reviewers will demand one.

Phase 3: build with -Dcassert=false -Ddebug=false (release-equivalent) for benchmarking; NEVER benchmark with cassert. Separately, run check-world on the assertion build.

Phase 4 (focus):
  - Reproduce on the same hardware, multiple runs (median of >=3, ideally pgbench -T 60 -j N -c M or similar).
  - Master vs patched, same flags, same data.
  - Regression hunt on unrelated workloads: pgbench tpc-b plus a few representative join queries the patch didn't target.
  - Memory: did you trade CPU for memory? Watch RSS.
  - Any new O(N^2)? Try larger inputs — a faster constant factor that hides quadratic growth is a footgun.
  - Microbenchmark vs end-to-end: a 30% speedup on a hot inner loop may be 2% end-to-end on real workloads. Measure both and be honest in the cover letter.

Phase 5: readability shouldn't suffer for perf. Comments must explain WHY this is faster (cache locality, branch prediction, fewer allocations, …). No leftover debug elog. `git diff --check` clean.

Phase 6: any data-structure layout change? If yes, ABI / catversion / WAL-magic implications.

Phase 7 (self-review specifically): tests cover corner cases not just happy path; commit history split into logical units (refactor first, optimization second); no debug elog(WARNING); no commented-out code; catversion / WAL magic / control version bumps where needed.

In the cover letter: post your numbers as a table — master tps, patched tps, run count, hardware, build flags, exact repro recipe. Distinguish blocking-quality numbers from nice-to-have observations.
evals.baseline: Build in release mode (no cassert). Reproduce the benchmark multiple times on the same hardware and take the median. Compare master vs patched with identical flags and data. Look for regressions on workloads you didn't target — run pgbench tpc-b and a few representative queries. Measure end-to-end impact, not just the inner-loop micro number, since a 30% hot-loop speedup might be 2% in real queries. Post the exact recipe and your numbers in the cover letter so others can reproduce. Make sure tests still pass on an assertion build separately, and that there's no debug elog left behind.
